title: Testing — Code You Can Trust
subtitle: Unit, integration, and end-to-end tests — the testing pyramid explained

sections.title: The Testing Pyramid
sections.text:
The testing pyramid is a framework for organizing tests by scope and speed. Tests at the bottom are fast and numerous; tests at the top are slow and few.

  • Unit Tests (Base) — Test individual functions, classes, or methods in isolation. Mock external dependencies. Run in milliseconds. Thousands of them.
  • Integration Tests (Middle) — Test how components work together (database queries, API calls, file system). Slower but catch real bugs.
  • End-to-End Tests (Top) — Test the entire system from the user's perspective. Browser automation (Playwright, Cypress, Selenium). Slow and brittle but validate critical user journeys.

The shape of the pyramid represents quantity: write lots of unit tests, some integration tests, and a few E2E tests.
sections.caption: The testing pyramid: many unit tests, some integration tests, few E2E tests

sections.title: Writing Testable Code
sections.text:
Code that's easy to test is well-designed code. Follow these principles:

  • Dependency Injection — Pass dependencies (database, API client) as parameters instead of hard-coding them. This lets you inject mocks in tests.
  • Pure Functions — Functions that return the same output for the same input and have no side effects. Trivially testable.
  • Single Responsibility — Each function does one thing. Simple functions are easy to test.
  • Interface Segregation — Don't make functions depend on things they don't use. Smaller interfaces = easier mocking.

If a function is hard to test, it's a sign that the design needs improvement.

items.heading: Dependency Injection
items.body: Pass deps as params → mock them in tests. Never import directly.

items.heading: Pure Functions
items.body: Same input = same output. No side effects. Trivially testable.

items.heading: Mocks & Stubs
items.body: Replace real deps with test doubles. Mock external APIs, databases.

items.heading: Coverage
items.body: Measure line/branch coverage. 80% is good, 100% isn't always necessary.

sections.caption: Testable code principles — DI, pure functions, and proper mocking

sections.title: CI/CD Test Pipeline
sections.text:
In a professional environment, tests run automatically on every push via Continuous Integration (CI). GitHub Actions, GitLab CI, and CircleCI are popular tools.

A typical test pipeline:

  1. Developer pushes code to a branch
  2. CI server picks up the change
  3. Installs dependencies (npm install)
  4. Runs linters (npm run lint)
  5. Runs unit tests (npm test)
  6. Runs integration tests (with test database)
  7. Builds the application
  8. If all steps pass: deploy or merge

This catches bugs before they reach production. A red CI should block merging until fixed.
sections.caption: CI pipeline: every push triggers automated checks before code is merged
sections.code:
# GitHub Actions workflow (.github/workflows/ci.yml)
name: CI
on: [push, pull_request]

jobs:
  test:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - uses: actions/setup-node@v4
        with:
          node-version: '20'
      - run: npm ci
      - run: npm run lint
      - run: npm test
      - run: npm run build
sections.callout: CI should be fast (<10 minutes). If tests are slow, they'll be skipped. Pre-commit hooks can catch simple issues before CI even runs.
